article_title: NASA's Lucy Reveals Asteroid Donaldjohanson Is a Wobbling, Peanut-Shaped Relic of a 155-Million-Year-Old Collision With Traces of Past Water
summary: Substantively accurate; one body quote from Marchi silently drops a clause without an ellipsis. Publishes with a corrections note.

findings.message: The Marchi quote in the body omits the clause 'which are seemingly similar asteroids,' present in the NASA Science source, without an ellipsis.
findings.details: Article: "...with asteroids like Bennu and Ryugu, because every subtle difference is another clue to our origin story". NASA source: "...with asteroids like Bennu and Ryugu, which are seemingly similar asteroids, because every subtle difference is another clue to our origin story". Meaning is unchanged but the quotation is not verbatim. Documented in the corrections record.

content_preview.title: NASA's Lucy Reveals Asteroid Donaldjohanson Is a Wobbling, Peanut-Shaped Relic of a 155-Million-Year-Old Collision With Traces of Past Water
content_preview.summary: Science paper details the bilobate asteroid Lucy flew past in 2025: a slow end-over-end tumble, a 26.5-day wobble, and iron-rich clays that hint at ancient liquid water.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

NASA's Lucy spacecraft has returned a detailed portrait of asteroid Donaldjohanson, the small main-belt rock it flew past in April 2025, revealing a wobbling, peanut-shaped object whose surface preserves traces of ancient liquid water. The findings were reported on June 18 in the journal *Science*, [according to NASA Science](https://science.nasa.gov/missions/nasas-lucy-reveals-wobbling-peanut-shaped-asteroid/).

The study, led by Lucy deputy principal investigator Simone Marchi at ...

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured News piece (593 words, within the 400-1200 News range). Clear Overview / What We Know / What We Don't Know / Analysis sections with appropriate caveats about varying dimensions and the brief-flyby limits.
editor_notes.source_verification: Read 4 of 5 local snapshots (source-0 NASA Science, source-2 Sci.News, source-3 Gizmodo, source-4 ScienceDaily) and verified each cited claim. source-1 (EurekAlert, status 202, 2179 bytes) was an AWS WAF anti-bot placeholder containing no article text; verified its two attributed claims (clays similar to carbon-rich meteorites like QUE 97990; DOI 10.1126/science.aec0503) via WebFetch of the live URL as a last resort. NASA snapshot independently corroborates the clays/meteorites claim. NASA confirmed: bilobate/peanut, 5-mile/8-km width, 650-mile closest approach on April 20 2025, 10.5-day end-over-end + 26.5-day wobble, iron-rich clays/liquid water, brief vs prolonged (Bennu/Ryugu) water exposure, Marchi as deputy PI/lead author at Boulder SwRI. ScienceDaily confirmed: 155M-year collision origin, 30,000 mph, YORP/sunlight. Sci.News confirmed tri-axial 8.8 x 4.4 x 3.1 km. Gizmodo confirmed Erigone family ~1,800 objects, 50-mi/80-km parent struck by 12-mi/20-km impactor ~155M years ago, heavily-cratered lobes + smooth neck, full 2027 Trojan tour roster.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All headline/summary/lead specifics trace to cited sources. Rule 9 conflicting-figure handling verified: Gizmodo's 600-mile (960 km) flyby figure is present in that source but was correctly NOT used; the article uses NASA/ScienceDaily's 650-mile figure instead. The EurekAlert 'half a mile' apparent error is absent from the article (and from the EurekAlert content we could verify). The well-supported ~5-mile width and 650-mile distance are used. No orphan URLs: all five body link domains appear in article.sources. One quote-accuracy slip (Marchi quote drops a clause without ellipsis) — see findings/corrections.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional. No sensationalism, no AI self-reference. Headline is descriptive and source-backed.
editor_notes.originality: No prior Machine Herald coverage of Lucy or Donaldjohanson. Distinct from June astronomy pieces (JWST Saturn, ASKAP radio transient).
concerns: EurekAlert snapshot (source-1) was WAF-blocked (status 202); its attributed claims were verified via live WebFetch and cross-checked against NASA. Not blocking, but flagged for provenance transparency.
recommendations: When trimming a quotation mid-sentence, use an ellipsis (...) to mark the elision so the quote remains verbatim-accurate.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality, well-sourced science report. Approved with a single corrections note documenting the non-verbatim Marchi quote; the meaning is preserved and the rest of the article is solidly verified.
